title.en: The Farewell Pilgrimage: The Complete Story

description: Tracing the detailed path of the Prophet ﷺ during his final Hajj, from his departure in Madinah to the final return, detailing every sacred rite.

text.en: The year was the 10th of Hijrah. Rasulullah ﷺ announced his intention to lead the Hajj, drawing an unimaginable crowd of over 100,000 companions.
On Saturday, the 25th of Dhul Qadah, after appointing Abu Dujana al-Ansari as governor, the massive caravan set forth from Madinah after the Zuhr prayer.
They made their first stop at Zul Hulaifa to spend the night. The following day, the Prophet ﷺ bathed and donned his ihram, which consisted of two simple pieces of unsewn, white Yemeni cotton.
Mounting his she-camel, Al Qaswa, he chanted the Talbiyah aloud, signaling the official commencement of the pilgrimage.
The long journey to Makkah was filled with profound teachings. At the valley of Rowhaa, the Prophet ﷺ revealed that seventy Prophets had performed their prayers in that very spot.
When Abu Bakr grew furious over a slave losing their luggage, the Prophet ﷺ gently smiled and reminded him of the peaceful and patient state required while in ihram.

title.en: Zi Tuwa: Nearing the Sacred City

text.en: Nearing the holy city, the caravan spent the night at Zi Tuwa.
On Sunday, the 4th of Dhul Hijjah, the Prophet ﷺ performed ghusl once more in the morning to prepare for his official entrance into Makkah.
The caravan entered Makkah and went straight to the Masjid al-Haram.
Approaching the Kaaba, the Prophet ﷺ commenced the sacred rites by touching and kissing the Hajarul Aswad.
He then proceeded to circumambulate the Kaaba seven times for his Tawaf.
Afterward, he performed a two-rak'ah prayer at Maqaam-e-Ibrahim, reciting Surah Al Kaafiroon and Surah Ikhlaas.
Following his prayers at the Kaaba, the Prophet ﷺ went to the blessed Zamzam well.
He drank deeply from its water, quenching his thirst after the long desert journey from Madinah.
He then continued to the hills of Safa and Marwah to complete the Sa'ee, the ritual walking between the two mountains, seven times.
Because he was performing Hajj Qiran, he kept his ihram on after completing this initial Umrah rite.
After resting in Makkah for four days, the immense sea of pilgrims relocated to Mina on the morning of the 8th of Dhul Hijjah.
They remained there and performed five daily prayers in total, from Zuhr until Fajr the following day.
On Friday morning, the 9th of Dhul Hijjah, the procession marched toward the majestic plain of Arafat.
The Prophet ﷺ rested in a tent erected specifically for him at Namirah on the east side of the mount until the sun passed its zenith.
Riding his camel into the valley of Uranah, the Prophet ﷺ delivered his historic Farewell Sermon to the awe-struck assembly.
He spoke with immense urgency and love, heavily emphasizing the sanctity of a Muslim's blood, property, and honor, warning them that they might not see him again.
He then moved to the plain of Arafat, spending the afternoon until sunset in tearful, humble supplication for his Ummah.
Here, the heavy revelation descended: 'This day I have perfected your religion for you.' The weight of this divine message was so immense that his camel was forced to sit down.
After sunset, they traveled swiftly to Muzdalifah. The Prophet ﷺ combined his Maghrib and Isha prayers and rested for the night.
Recognizing the immense crowd, he compassionately sent the women, children, and elderly ahead to Mina to avoid the rush.
At dawn, the caravan departed Muzdalifah. Along the way, his companion Fadhl picked up the small pebbles needed for the stoning ritual.
They drove through Wadi-e-Muhassar with haste, as it was a place where Allah's wrath had once descended upon Abraha's army of elephants.
Arriving at Mina on the morning of the 10th of Dhul Hijjah, they went straight to Jamaratul Aqaba.
The Prophet ﷺ threw stones at the pillar, reciting the takbir with each throw, which marked the end of the continuous recitation of the Talbiyah.
Still in Mina, the Prophet ﷺ sacrificed 63 camels with his own hands—one for every year of his blessed life—while Sayyidina Ali slaughtered the rest to total 100.
He then had his head shaved, his nails trimmed, applied scent, and finally changed out of his ihram clothing.
At the time of Zuhr, the Prophet ﷺ traveled the short distance back to Makkah to perform the Tawaf-uz-Ziyaarah.
This completed an essential pillar of the Hajj, after which he returned to Mina.
For the next three days, the 11th, 12th, and 13th of Dhul Hijjah, known as the Days of Tashreeq, the Prophet ﷺ remained in Mina.
Each day, after the sun passed its zenith, he threw stones at the three Jamaraat, completing the stoning rituals.
On Tuesday, the 13th of Dhul Hijjah, the caravan finally left Mina and headed towards Makkah.
They stopped to spend the night outside Makkah at Mahaab, also called Bathaa or Khaif Bani Kinana—ironically, the very spot where the Quraysh had once signed a pact to boycott and kill him years ago.
That evening at Mahaab, his wife Aisha was deeply saddened because she had been unable to perform her initial Umrah due to menstruation.
The Prophet ﷺ comforted her and sent her with her brother to Tanaim so she could initiate and complete her Umrah.
After resting, the Prophet ﷺ led his companions into Makkah for one final rite: the Farewell Tawaf.
With this circumambulation completed, the rites of Hajj were officially perfected, and the command was given to head home.
The long journey back to Madinah began, and on the 18th of Dhul Hijjah, the massive caravan stopped at Ghadeer-Khum.
The Prophet ﷺ ascended a lofty place to deliver another profound sermon, particularly praising the virtues of Sayyidina Ali.
After spending their final night on the road at Zul Hulaifa, the pilgrims performed their Fajr prayer.
They triumphantly re-entered Madinah, bringing the historic Farewell Pilgrimage to a close and leaving an eternal Sunnah for the Ummah.
